risk: Systemic, project-wide schema-vs-practice drift discovered by exhaustively validating every verification.json and handoff.json under .cdr/runs/*/*/ (excluding metadata.json, which conforms almost universally: 455/456) against their respective JSON Schemas. Result: only 3 of 192 verification.json files validate against verification.schema.json (which requires schema_version, verdict, findings -- but the organically-adopted real convention across 184/192 runs uses subtask+dimensions [plural, not the schema's singular 'dimension' key typo] with schema_version present in only 91/192 and no top-level 'findings' array at all in the vast majority). Only 3 of 340 handoff.json files validate against handoff.schema.json (which requires from_agent/to_agent/run_id/context_pointers -- real convention instead uses ad hoc fields like 'pointers', 'subtask', 'commit', varying per agent/run). This is not a handful of stragglers; it means these two schemas have effectively never been the real, enforced contract for the vast majority of this entire project's history, despite index.lines.schema.json and run.metadata.schema.json being genuinely conformed-to almost universally.
recommendation: Not fixed in this pass -- deliberately scoped out, matching the precedent set for the docs/LLD staleness gap (pending.md's 'scope-sized gap, not a single-commit fix' framing): reconciling two schemas against 500+ historical artifacts (or vice versa) is a genuine design decision (which convention is authoritative going forward?) rather than a simple hygiene correction, and .cdr/runs/* itself is out of scope to edit per this audit's own instructions. Recommend a dedicated future task to either (a) rewrite verification.schema.json/handoff.schema.json to match the real, organically-adopted field conventions (dimensions/subtask/verdict for verification; pointers/subtask/commit-style for handoff), formalizing what already works in practice, or (b) decide the schemas are the actual target contract and add a migration/lint step. Does not block milestone #10 -- no run in this session was ever actually gated on schema conformance (metadata.json, the one schema that IS conformed to almost universally, is the one actually load-bearing for run-directory bookkeeping/resumability).

finding: agents/eval/datasets.py performs sys.path mutation at import/call time for cross-root data/<->agents/ imports, copying a pattern from a test-only file (agents/ingestion/test_e2e_smoke.py) into production/library code. Idempotent and __file__-anchored so not immediately fragile, but should be replaced by a proper pythonpath/packaging fix before more modules copy the same pattern.

No test exercises limit=0 boundary on load_dataset's common interface (does 0 correctly yield zero records rather than being treated as falsy/no-limit?). Underlying loaders use index>=limit comparisons which should handle this correctly, but it is untested at either layer.

render_pdf's page-overflow loop in data/gen_synthetic_pdfs.py always breaks after a single fallback insert_textbox() attempt at 85% font size, never checking whether that attempt itself still overflowed. max_pages/multi-page handling is dead code and the documented RuntimeError safety net is unreachable, so a sufficiently long LLM completion would be silently truncated with no error and no test coverage. Not currently triggered in practice (sampled/live completions fit on one page).
recommendation: Check the fallback insert_textbox()'s return value; only clear remaining/break if it succeeded, otherwise genuinely add a page or raise the already-documented RuntimeError. Add a regression test with an artificially long fake-LLM completion to exercise the overflow path before scaling topics.yaml to 30-50 entries.

finding: Independent re-spot-check (seed=7, 5 topics: social-media-conduct, change-management, gift-and-entertainment, incident-response, vendor-onboarding) using fitz text extraction found the same class of additive LLM hallucination already disclosed by the implementer (invented non-seeded policy names beyond real seeded cross-references), but at higher density than disclosed: doc-change-management.pdf invents 'Emergency Response Policy' and doc-vendor-onboarding.pdf invents THREE non-seeded policy names ('Security and Compliance Policy', 'Liability Insurance Policy', 'Data Retention and Disposal Policy'). vendor-onboarding was one of the implementer's own seed-42 sample of 7 topics, yet their self-consistency.json discrepancies_found list only cites whistleblower-protection and data-subject-rights -- meaning the implementer's own spot-check missed a real, visible hallucination in a document they themselves reviewed. Severity is unchanged from the already-disclosed defect class (additive hallucination only; no seeded ground-truth claim was found to be false in either the implementer's sample or this independent one), but the implementer's disclosed '2/7' count understates actual prevalence and the spot-check process has a demonstrated miss.
recommendation: No code fix required (ground_truth.py's structural derivation remains correct -- the hallucination lives in LLM-generated PDF prose, not in the ground-truth labels). If this benchmark is used for anything beyond a demonstrative harness, consider automating the spot-check (e.g. flag any policy-name-shaped phrase in rendered text not present in topics.yaml) rather than relying on manual seed-sampled review, since manual review demonstrably missed an instance within its own sample.

finding: Implementer's self-consistency.json claims 'agents/ full regression: 363 passed, 8 failed + 2 collection errors, all attributable to a pre-existing protobuf gencode 6.33.5 vs runtime 5.29.6 mismatch, independently reproduced via git stash of every file this commit touches.' Independently re-running the full agents/ suite in this verification pass (same git HEAD, same venv) produced 374 passed, 0 failed, 0 collection errors -- both WITH the commit's changes present and after manually reverting every file the commit touches (topics.yaml, ground_truth.py, test_ground_truth.py, generated/*.pdf+manifest.json, ground_truth.json) via git checkout, confirming zero regression either way. The installed protobuf package is now 6.33.6 (not the 5.29.6 the implementer reported), i.e. the shared venv's protobuf version has drifted/been upgraded since the implementer's session, eliminating the gencode/runtime mismatch entirely. This means the implementer's specific claimed failure count could not be independently reproduced or falsified as stated -- it is neither confirmed nor contradicted, just no longer observable in the current environment. Net effect on this verification: definitively no regression from this diff (0 failures with and without the changes), but the specific 'pre-existing and unrelated, 8 failures' narrative rests on environment state that has since changed and should not be cited as still-current without re-verification.
recommendation: Track shared-venv protobuf/grpcio pinning explicitly (a lockfile or CI-pinned version) so environment drift between agent sessions cannot silently change regression-suite pass/fail counts and undermine future git-stash-based 'pre-existing and unrelated' claims.

finding: select_chunk_config's grid search over 9 (chunk_size, overlap) candidates is genuinely exercised (independently re-run by this verification pass, reproducing self-consistency.json's recall table exactly, including the one discriminating result (220,33)=0.9). However, 8/9 candidates tied at perfect recall@3 on the small 5-doc/5-query fixture corpus, meaning the shipped defaults (80 words, 0% overlap) were effectively selected by the tie-break rule (prefer smaller chunk size, then smaller overlap) rather than by any empirical demonstration that 0% overlap is competitive. The tie-break rule's direction mechanically favors the leanest/weakest config whenever the corpus fails to discriminate, which is the same direction a strawman implementation would land on by default even though this implementation reached it via a legitimate, reproducible, and honestly-disclosed process. Not itself a defect (0%-overlap chunking is a legitimate real-world baseline choice, not inherently a strawman), but nothing in the module enforces that a future real-corpus benchmark subtask re-run this sweep before treating these fixture-tuned defaults as final for comparative HiveMind-vs-baseline conclusions.
recommendation: Before any subtask wires this module into the real Bitext/Enron/synthetic-PDF benchmark corpus (e.g. a 5.3.4-style subtask), require it to re-run select_chunk_config against the real corpus rather than importing DEFAULT_CHUNK_SIZE_WORDS/DEFAULT_OVERLAP_WORDS uncritically. Consider documenting or reconsidering the prefer-leanest tie-break direction given its systematic bias toward the weaker candidate whenever ties occur.

finding: Implementer's self-consistency.json claims 'full agents/ test suite: 386 passed in ~227s, zero regressions.' Independently re-running the full agents/ suite in this verification pass (same git HEAD) produced 8 failures plus 2 collection errors, all in ingestion/test_e2e_smoke.py, ingestion/test_shortlist.py, query/test_server.py, query/test_wiring.py, and test_hivemind_pb2_smoke.py, all due to a 'google.protobuf.runtime_version.VersionError: gencode 6.33.5 vs runtime 5.29.6' mismatch. None of the failing modules are touched by this diff, and this is the same class of protobuf gencode/runtime drift previously flagged in subtask 5.1.3's verification (run 10006-verification) -- the shared venv's protobuf version has evidently drifted again since. Confirmed via git stash/restore that this failure pattern is present regardless of this commit's changes (pre-existing, not a regression introduced by 573478a). eval/ subset (59 tests) and the target test_vector_rag_baseline.py (12 tests) both independently re-run and passed 100% in isolation.
recommendation: Same as the 5.1.3 finding: pin protobuf/grpcio versions in a lockfile or CI check so shared-venv drift stops silently invalidating full-suite regression claims across subtasks.

finding: DEFAULT_EXTRA_CANDIDATES=2 / candidate_pool_size=3 are fixture-scale constants (disclosed in module docstring); a future full-synthetic-corpus benchmark run must re-tune the candidate pool size rather than reusing the fixture default unmodified.

finding: _match_query_entities' substring-containment paraphrase-tolerance fallback (agents/eval/baselines/graphrag_lite.py) has no minimum-length or stopword guard, unlike the sibling _heuristic_entities fallback which does. Independently reproduced a false-positive cross-document match: a query entity 'a' (single char) matches both 'apple' and 'banana' via substring containment, retrieving both unrelated documents. Realistic LLM-extracted short entities (2-4 chars, or coincidental substrings) could trigger the same false-positive pattern at real-corpus scale, inflating recall in a way that is not genuinely graph/semantic-driven -- a fairness risk in the 'too permissive' direction. No existing test (unit/stub/live) exercises this failure direction; only the intended-positive paraphrase case is tested.
recommendation: Add a minimum canonical-entity-length guard to the substring fallback (mirroring _MIN_HEURISTIC_ENTITY_WORDS intent) or require meaningful word-token overlap rather than raw character containment. Add a negative/adversarial test proving the fallback does not over-match on short/coincidental substrings before this baseline is wired to a real corpus (issue #28 or later).
